concepts.title: Fluids
concepts.conceptBody:
Fluids

The substances which flow are called fluids. Both liquids and gases come in this category. Now, let's have a deeper view of the difference between solids and fluids. Solid have the property of deformation when subjected to shear stress but deformation is constant for a particular stress. While fluids continue to deform until stress is withdrawn.

concepts.title: Density
concepts.conceptBody:
Density 

Density of a substance is defined as the ratio of its mas & volume. Let a substance be of mass $\text{M}$ which has a volume ${{\text{V}}_{1}}$ then density of the substance is given by

$\rho =\frac{M}{V}$

Unit of density is $\text{kg}/{{\text{m}}^{3}}$ in S.I. system.

concepts.title: Relative Density
concepts.conceptBody:
Relative Density or specific gravity 

Relative density of a substance is defined as the ratio of the density of the substance to the density of pure water at ${{4}^{0}}\text{c}$.

${{\rho }_{\text{r}}}=\rho /{{\rho }_{\text{w}}}$

concepts.title: Density of Mixture of Substance in Proportion of Mass
concepts.conceptBody:
Density of Mixture of Substance in Proportion of Mass

Let a number of substances of masses ${{M}_{1}},{{M}_{2}},{{M}_{3}}$ etc. and densities ${{\rho }_{1}},{{\rho }_{2}},{{\rho }_{3}}$ etc. respectively are mixed together. The total mass of the mixture.

$\begin{array}{l} {} ={{M}_{1}}+{{M}_{2}}+{{M}_{3}}\ldots \ldots \\ \text{ total }\text{ volume } =\frac{{{M}_{1}}}{{{\rho }_{1}}}+\frac{{{M}_{2}}}{{{\rho }_{2}}}+\frac{{{M}_{3}}}{{{\rho }_{3}}} \\ \end{array}$

Therefore, the density of mixture is

$S=\frac{{{M}_{1}}+{{M}_{2}}+{{M}_{3}}\ldots }{\frac{{{M}_{1}}}{{{\rho }_{1}}}+\frac{{{M}_{2}}}{{{\rho }_{2}}}+\frac{{{M}_{3}}}{{{\rho }_{3}}}+\ldots .}$

For two substances the density of the mixture can be written as

$\rho =\frac{{{\rho }_{1}}{{\rho }_{2}}\left( {{M}_{1}}+{{M}_{2}} \right)}{{{\rho }_{1}}{{M}_{1}}+{{\rho }_{2}}{{M}_{2}}}$

concepts.title: Density of Mixture of Substance in Proportion of Volume
concepts.conceptBody:
Density of Mixture of Substance in Proportion of Volume

Suppose that a number of substances of volume ${{V}_{1}},{{V}_{2}},{{V}_{3}}$ etc. and densities ${{\rho }_{1}},{{\rho }_{2}},{{\rho }_{3}}$, etc. respectively are mixed together. The total mass of the mixture.

$\begin{array}{*{35}{r}} {} & ~={{\rho }_{1}}\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{\text{V}}_{1}}+{{\rho }_{2}}\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{\text{V}}_{2}}+{{\rho }_{3}}\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{\text{V}}_{3}}\ldots \ldots .. \\ {} & {} \\ \end{array}$

$\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ total }\!\!~\!\!\text{ volume }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$$~={{\text{V}}_{1}}+{{\text{V}}_{2}}+{{\text{V}}_{3}}\ldots \ldots $

Therefore, density of the mixture is

$\begin{array}{*{35}{r}} {} & \rho =\frac{{{\rho }_{1}}{{V}_{1}}+{{\rho }_{2}}{{V}_{2}}+{{\rho }_{3}}{{V}_{3}}\cdots }{{{V}_{1}}+{{V}_{2}}+{{V}_{3}}\cdots } \\ {} & {} \\ \end{array}$

For two substances $\rho =\frac{{{\rho }_{1}}{{V}_{1}}+{{\rho }_{2}}{{V}_{2}}}{{{V}_{1}}+{{V}_{2}}}$

concepts.title: Pressure
concepts.conceptBody:
Pressure

If we fill a vessel having a hole with fluid, fluid flows out of the hole. Now if we cover this hole with a plate, which exactly fits the hole, the place can remain at rest only if we apply some external force on the plate. This shows that fluid exerts force on the plate to push it outwards. If $\text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ s}$ is the area of the plate and $\text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ F}$ is the normal force exerted by the fluid, the pressure at the hole

$\text{p}=\underset{\text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ S}\to }{\mathop{\text{lim}}}\,\frac{\text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ F}}{\text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ s}}=\frac{\text{dF}}{\text{ds}}$

For a point at dept $\text{h}$below the surface of a liquid density $\rho $, hydrostatic pressure $\text{p}$ is given by :

$\text{p}={{\text{p}}_{0}}+\text{h}\rho \text{g}$

where ${{\text{p}}_{0}}$ is the atmospheric pressure. The pressure difference between hydrostatic pressure and atmospheric pressure is called gauge-pressure and will be

$\text{p}-{{\text{p}}_{0}}=\text{h}\rho \text{g}$

Regarding pressure it is worth noting that:

(1) Even though pressure is produced by a force that has directional properties and is a vector, the pressure itself is a scalar as its direction (always normal to the area considered) is unique and not to be specified.

(2) It has dimensions $\left[ \text{M}{{\text{L}}^{-1}}{{\text{T}}^{-2}} \right]$ and $\text{SI}$ unit $\text{N}/{{\text{m}}^{2}}$ which is specifically called Pascal (Pa). Other practical units of pressure are atmosphere, bar and torr ($\text{mm}$ of $\text{Hg}$) with

$1\text{ atm}=1.01 \times {{10}^{5}}\text{ Pa}=1.01\text{bar}=760\text{ torr }$liquid

(3) At a point it acts in all directions. If a pressure measuring device is at a given point in a fluid, whatever be its orientation the pressure remains the same.

(4) It always acts normal to the fluid boundaries as the ability to flow makes fluid unable to sustain a tangential force.

(5) It depends on the depth of the point below the surface (h), nature of liquid ( $\rho $ ) and acceleration due to gravity $\left( \text{g} \right)$ while it is independent of the amount of liquid, shape of the container or cross sectional area considered. So if a given liquid is filled in vessels of different shapes to same height, the pressure at the base in each vessel's will be the same, though the volume or weight of the liquid in different vessels will be different. 
 

${{\text{p}}_{\text{A}}}={{\text{p}}_{\text{B}}}={{\text{p}}_{\text{C}}}\text{ }\text{ but }\text{ }{{\text{W}}_{\text{A}}}<{{\text{W}}_{\text{B}}}<{{\text{W}}_{\text{C}}}$

(6) In a liquid at same level, the pressure will be same at all points; if not, due to pressure difference the liquid cannot be at rest. This is why the height of luqid is the same in vessels of different shapes containing different amounts of the same liquid at rest when they are in communication with each other.

concepts.title: Pressure Difference In Accelerating Fluids
concepts.conceptBody:
Pressure Difference in Accelerating Fluids

Consider a liquid kept at rest in a beaker as shown in figure (a). In this case we know that pressure do not change in horizontal direction (x-direction), it decreases upwards along y-direction.

So, we can write the equations.

$\frac{dP}{dx}=0 \text{ and }\frac{dP}{dy}=-\rho g$ … (i)

But suppose the beaker is accelerated and it has components of acceleration ${{\text{a}}_{\text{x}}}$ and ${{\text{a}}_{\text{y}}}$ in $\text{x}$ and $\text{y}$ directions respectively, then the pressure decreases along both $\text{x}$ and $\text{y}$ directions. The above equation in that case reduces to,

$\frac{dP}{dx}=-\rho {{a}_{x}}\text{ and }\frac{dP}{dy}=-\rho \left( g+{{a}_{y}} \right)$ … (ii)

 

These equations can be derived as under:

Consider a beaker filled with some liquid of density $\text{p}$ accelerating upwards with an acceleration ${{\text{a}}_{\text{y}}}$ along positive y-direction. Let us draw the free body diagram of a small element of fluid of area A and length dy as shown in figure.

Equation of motion for this fluid element is,

$\text{PA}-\text{W}-\left( \text{P}+\text{dP} \right)\text{A}=\left( \text{mass} \right)\left( {{\text{a}}_{\text{y}}} \right)$

Or $-\text{W}-\left( \text{dP} \right)\text{A}=\left( \text{A}\rho \text{dy} \right)\left( {{\text{a}}_{\text{y}}} \right)$

Or $-\left( \text{A}\rho \text{gdy} \right)-\left( \text{dP} \right)\text{A}=\left( \text{A}\rho \text{dy} \right)\left( {{\text{a}}_{\text{y}}} \right)$

 

Or $\frac{dP}{dy}=-\rho \left( g+{{a}_{y}} \right)$

Similarly, if the beaker moves along positive $\text{x}$ direction with acceleration ${{\text{a}}_{\text{x}}}$, the equation of motion for the fluid element shown in figure is,

$\text{PA}-\left( \text{P}+\text{dP} \right)\text{A}=\left( \text{mass} \right)\left( {{\text{a}}_{\text{x}}} \right)$

Or $-\left( dP \right)A=\left( A\rho dx \right){{a}_{x}}$

Or $\frac{\text{dP}}{\text{dx}}=-\rho {{a}_{x}}$

concepts.title: Free Surface of Accelerating Fluid in Horizontal
concepts.conceptBody:
Free Surface of a Liquid Accelerated in Horizontal Direction 

 

Consider a liquid placed in a beaker which is accelerating horizontally with an acceleration 'a'. Let $\text{A}$ and $\text{B}$ be two points in the liquid at a separation $\text{x}$ in the same horizontal line. As we have seen in this case.

$\frac{\text{dP}}{\text{dx}}=-\rho \text{a}$

Or $\text{dP}$ $=-\rho \text{a}$ $\text{dx}$

Integrating this with proper limits, we get

${{\text{P}}_{\text{A}}}-{{\text{P}}_{\text{B}}}=\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ pax }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$ … (iii)

Further, ${{\text{P}}_{\text{A}}}={{\text{P}}_{0}}+\rho g\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{\text{h}}_{1}}$

${{\text{P}}_{\text{B}}}={{\text{P}}_{0}}+\rho g\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{\text{h}}_{2}}$

Substituting, in Eq. (iii), we get

$\rho g\left( {{h}_{1}}-{{h}_{2}} \right)$ $~=\rho ax$

$\frac{{{h}_{1}}-{{h}_{2}}}{x}$ $~=\frac{a}{g}=\text{tan}\theta $

$\text{tan}\theta $ $~=\frac{a}{g}$
